title: - AQUILA: COMMUNICATION EFFICIENT FEDER ATED LEARNING WITH ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - AQUILA: COMMUNICATION EFFICIENT FEDER ATED LEARNING WITH ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The development and deployment of federated learning (FL) have been bottlenecked by the heavy communication overheads of high-dimensional models between the distributed client nodes and the central server. To achieve better errorcommunication tradeoffs, recent efforts have been made to either adaptively reduce the communication frequency by skipping unimportant updates, a.k.a. lazilyaggregated quantization (LAQ), or adjust the quantization bits for each communication. In this paper, we propose a unifying communication efficient framework for FL based on **a** daptive **qu** ant **i** zation of **l** azily- **a** ggregated gradients ( **AQUILA** ), which adaptively adjusts two mutually-dependent factors, the communication frequency and the quantization level, in a synergistic way. Specifically, we start from a careful investigation on the classical LAQ scheme and formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem where the optimal quantization level per communication is selected by minimizing the gradient loss caused by updates skipping. Meanwhile, we adjust the LAQ strategy to better fit the novel quantization criterion and thus keep the communication frequency at an appropriate level. The effectiveness and convergence of the proposed AQUILA framework are theoretically verified. The experimental results demonstrate that AQUILA can reduce around **50%** of overall transmitted bits compared to existing methods while achieving the same level of model accuracy in a number of non-homogeneous FL scenarios, including Non-IID data distribution and heterogeneous model architecture. The proposed AQUILA is highly adaptive and compatible to existing FL settings.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

With the deployment of ubiquitous sensing and computing devices, the Internet of things (IoT) as well as many other distributed systems have gradually grown from concept to reality, bringing dramatic convenience to people’s daily life (Du et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020a; Hard et al., 2018). To fully utilize such distributed computing resources, distributed learning provides a promising framework that can achieve comparable performance with the traditional centralized learning scheme. However, the privacy and security of sensitive data during the updating and transmitting processes in distributed learning have been a growing concern. In this context, _federated learning (FL)_ (McMahan et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019) has been developed, enabling distributed devices to collaboratively learn a global model without privacy leakage by keeping private data sets isolated and masking transmitted information with secure approaches like differential privacy (Abadi et al., 2016), secret sharing techniques (Bonawitz et al., 2017) and homomorphic encryption (Liu et al., 2020b). Due to its privacy and security preserving property and great potentials in some distributed but privacysensitive fields like finance and health, FL has attracted tremendous attentions from both academia and industry in recent years.

Unfortunately, in many FL applications like image classification and objective recognition, the models to be trained tend to be high-dimensional, which lead to heavy communication overheads, for example, a Resnet-152 network has over 58 million parameters (He et al., 2016). Hence, communication efficiency has become one of the key bottlenecks of FL. To this end, recent researches have tried to reduce the communication frequency, for example, Sun et al. (2020) proposes the lazily-aggregated quantization (LAQ) method to reduce communication rounds by skipping some

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

unnecessary parameter uploads. To further reduce transmitted bits per communication, LAQ can be used jointly with gradient compression techniques, e.g. quantization and sparsification (Strom, 2015; Wangni et al., 2018; Lin et al., 2018; Han et al., 2020). Moreover, Mao et al. (2021) develops the Adaptive Quantized Gradient (AQG) for LAQ to adjust the quantization bit among multiple given levels during training. However, the AQG is not sufficiently adaptive, for example, in the two-level AQG with 4 bit and 2 bit, the situation of 3 bit and 1 bit is not covered at all. In a separate line of work, Jhunjhunwala et al. (2021) develops an adaptive quantization rule (AdaQuantFL) for FL which can search in a given range for an optimal quantization level and achieve a better error-communication tradeoff.

Previous work has investigated how to optimize communication frequency or adjust quantization levels in a highly adaptive fashion, but not both. Intuitively, we ask the question, _can we adaptively adjust the quantization level in LAQ to further reduce communication rounds and transmitted bits simultaneously?_ A straightforward approach is to train LAQ jointly with state-of-the-art adaptive quantization methods like AdaQuantFL. However, quantization level and communication frequency are mutually dependent in establishing the model convergence and must be adjusted together. For example, lazy aggregation leads to the skip of some gradients that no longer require quantization, while the choice of quantization levels directly affects the quality of a gradient update and thus whether it is selected for transmission and aggregation. Therefore, the key question of our work is how to jointly leverage these two complementary yet mutually-dependent degrees of freedom for further optimizing communication efficiency in FL.

The key idea of this paper is to select the optimal quantization bits for each communication round in LAQ by optimizing the gradient loss caused by skipping quantized updates, which gives a novel quantization criterion that can cooperate with LAQ strategy to further reduce overall transmitted bits while maintaining the desired convergence properties of LAQ. The contributions of this paper are trifold and summarized as follows. 1) We propose a FL framework with **a** daptive **qu** ant **i** zation of **l** azily- **a** ggregated gradients termed AQUILA, which simultaneously adjusts the communication frequency and quantization level in a synergistic fashion. 2) We formulate AQUILA as an optimization problem and develop an upperbound for the gradient loss caused by communication skipping, which gives a novel adaptive quantization criterion which is theoretically proven to be more efficient compared to AdaQuantFL while maintaining the same convergence properties. 3) We experimentally evaluate the performance of AQUILA in a number of non-homogeneous FL settings, including Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architecture. The experimental results show that AQUILA can significantly mitigate the communication overhead over a number of baselines including fixed-bit LAQ and the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL. Our approach is highly adaptive and compatible to existing FL settings.

## 2 BACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK

Consider a FL system with one central server and a clients set M of _M_ distributed clients to collaboratively train a global model parameterized by _**θ**[∗]_ . At iteration _k_ , each client _m ∈_ M first trains the global model _**θ**[k]_ on its local data _Dm[k]_[, and sends the local gradient] _**[ g]**[k] m_[=] _[∇][f][m]_[(] _[D] m[k]_[;] _**[ θ]**[k]_[)][ to the] central server. Then the server aggregates the parameters and updates the global parameter by:

To reduce communication overheads with gradient quantization, the stochastic uniform quantizer (Alistarh et al., 2017) is usually adopted. For any local gradient _**g** ∈_ R _[d]_ , the quantized value of its _i_ -th dimension with quantization level _b_ is defined as:

It is clear that with quantization level _b_ , the number of bits for transmitting a quantized gradient from a client to the central server is _Cb_ = _d ⌈_ log2( _b_ + 1) _⌉_ + _d_ + 32, with 32 bits for _∥_ _**g** ∥_ 2, 1 bit for each sign ( _gi_ ), and log2( _b_ + 1) bits for each _ξi_ ( _**g** , b_ ).

For communication rounds reduction, the LAQ proposes to let the client _m ∈_ M upload its newlyquantized local gradient _Qb_ ( _**g**[k] m_[)][ at iteration] _[ k]_[ only when the change in local gradient is sufficiently] large, i.e.,

where _Qb_ (ˆ _**g**[k] m[−]_[1] ) is the last quantized upload from client _m_ , _εb_ (ˆ _**g**[k] m[−]_[1] ) and _εb_ ( _**g**[k] m_[)][ denote quantiza-] tion errors, and _{ξd}[D] d_ =1[are some predetermined constant weights.][Notice here a] **[ fixed]**[ quantization] level _b_ is used. In LAQ, if the difference between client _m_ ’s newly-quantized local gradient _Qb_ ( _**g**[k] m_[)] and the last upload is smaller than a threshold involving quantization errors and global model’s innovation, client _m_ will skip the upload of _Qb_ ( _**g**[k] m_[)][at][iteration] _[k]_[and][the][central][server][will][reuses] _Qb_ (ˆ _**g**[k] m[−]_[1] ) for such lazy aggregation:

where M _[k]_ 0[denotes the subset of clients that skip the new gradient update and reuse the old quantized] gradient at iteration _k_ . Besides, _**g**_ ˆ _[k] m_[represents][the][actual][gradient][for][aggregation][from][client] _[m]_[,] which is _**g**[k] m_[for] _[ m][ ∈]_[M] _[ \]_[ M] _[k]_ 0[, while][ ˆ] _**[g]** m[k][−]_[1] for _m ∈_ M _[k]_ 0[.]

Recently, AdaQuantFL is proposed to achieve a better error-communication tradeoff by adaptively adjusting the quantization levels during FL training (Jhunjhunwala et al., 2021). Specifically, AdaQuantFL computes iteration _k_ ’s optimal quantization level _b[∗] k_[based on the following criterion] involving training loss and initial quantization level _b_ 0:
where _f_ ( _**θ**_[0] ) and _f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) are the global training loss at iteration 0 and _k_ , respectively.

However, AdaQuantFL transmits quantized gradients at **every iteration** . In order to skip unnecessary communication rounds and adaptively adjust quantization level for each communication jointly, an naive approach is to quantize lazily aggregated gradients with AdaQuantFL. Nevertheless, it fails to achieve efficient communication due to a number of reasons. Firstly, given the descending trend of training loss, AdaQuantFL’s criterion (6) may lead to high quantization bit number even exceeding 32 late in the training process, which is too much for cases where the global convergence is already approaching. Secondly, higher quantization level results in smaller quantization error, which will lead to lower communication threshold in LAQ’s criterion (4) and thus higher frequency of transmission. Therefore it is desirable to develop more efficient adaptive quantization method in the lazily-aggregated setting to systematically improve communication efficiency in FL.

## 3 METHOD

## 3.1 ADAPTIVE QUANTIZATION OF LAZILY-AGGREGATED GRADIENTS (AQUILA)

Given the above limitations of the naive joint use of existing adaptive quantization criterion and lazy aggregation strategy, this paper aims to design a unifying framework for communication efficiency optimization where the quantization level and communication frequency are adjusted in a synergistic and interactive way. Based on a careful investigation on LAQ, we design a novel quantization criterion where the optimal quantization level is selected by minimizing the expected gradient loss caused by skipping quantized updates. The rationale behind such strategy is, by formulating the adaptive quantization problem of lazily-aggregated gradients as optimizing the expected gradient loss with respect to the number of quantization bits, we can get an adaptive quantization criterion based on local gradient updates while maintaining or even improving the convergence property of

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

LAQ. To get the optimization target, we first derive an upperbound for the expected gradient loss in terms of quantization bits as elaborated in Section 3.2, which gives a novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) that selects the quantization level for client _m_ at iteration _k_ based on initial quantization level _b_ 0 and the change between client _m_ ’s newly computed gradient _gm[k]_[and][the][last][uploaded] gradient ˆ _gm[k][−]_[1] :

The superiority of (7) comes from the following two aspects. Firstly, the gradient updates tend to fluctuate along with the training process instead of keeping descending like the loss value, and thus prevent the quantization level from increasing tremendously compared with the initial level. Secondly, with lazy aggregation criterion based on gradient updates like (4), the transmitted bits in AQUILA is further controlled, since the gradient update for actual transmission in (7) is lower bounded by the lazy aggregation criterion, and therefore high-bit transmission for small update is more likely to be skipped.

To better fit the larger quantization errors induced by fewer quantization bits in (7), we modify the communication criterion as follows to avoid the potential expansion of clients group to be skipped:

Figure 1: The schematic illustration of the communication efficient FL with AQUILA in comparison with the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ. The blue lines indicating the transmission of quantized gradients in AQUILA are drawn in different thicknesses to represent different quantization levels selected by various clients.

The cooperation of the novel adaptive quantization criterion (7) and the modified lazy aggregation strategy (8) is illustrated in Fig. 1a. Compared to the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ where the mutual influence between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation has not been considered as shown in Fig. 1b, our AQUILA framework adaptively optimizes the allocation of quantization bits throughout training to promote the convergence of lazy aggregation, and at the same time utilizes the lazy aggregation strategy to improve the efficiency of adaptive quantization by skipping high-bit transmission. The proposed AQUILA’s effect of suppressing the transmission of high quantization bits has been verified in our experiments, as shown in Fig. 20 in the Appendix. Besides, with the adjusted lazy aggregation strategy (8), AQUILA well addresses the problem of high communication frequency in the late training process of naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ, as indicated by Fig. 2.

The proposed AQUILA is summarized as follows in Algorithm 1. At iteration _k_ = 0, all clients are forced to transmit local gradients quantized with the initial level _b_ 0. At iteration _k ∈{_ 1 _,_ 2 _, ..., K}_ , the server first broadcasts the global model _**θ**[k]_ to all clients. Each client _m_ computes _**g**[k] m_[with] local training data, and then uses it to select an optimal quantization level by (7). Then, each client

computes its gradient update after quantization and determines whether to upload the update or not based on the communication criterion (8). Finally, the server updates the new global model _**θ**[k]_[+1] with up-to-date quantized gradients _Qbkm_ ( _gm[k]_[)][ for those clients who send the uploads at iteration] _[ k]_[,] while reusing the old quantized gradients _Q_ ˆ _bkm−_ 1[(ˆ] _[g] m[k][−]_[1] ) for those who skip the uploads.

## 3.2 THEORETICAL DERIVATION AND ANALYSIS OF AQUILA

As mentioned before, in this work we bound the expected gradient loss caused by skipping updates with respect to quantization bits. Specifically, if the communication criterion (8) holds for client _m_ at iteration _k_ , it does not contribute to iteration _k_ ’s gradient loss, otherwise, the loss caused by client _m_ will be minimized with the optimal quantization criterion (7). In this section, the theoretical derivation of the target upper bound is based on following standard assumptions:
**Assumption 1.** _Loss function f_ ( _**θ**_ ) = � _m∈_ M _[f][m]_[(] _**[θ]**_[)] _[ is L-smooth, and][ f][m]_[(] _**[θ]**_[)] _[ is][ L][m][-smooth.]_

**Assumption 2.** _The quantization operation is unbiased with_ E[ _Qb_ ( _w_ ) _|w_ ] = _w, and its variance satisfy: ∀w ∈_ R _[d] ,_ E[ _∥Qb_ ( _w_ ) _− w∥_[2] 2 _[|][w]_[]] _[ ≤][q][b][ ∥][w][∥]_[2] 2 _[, where][ q][b][is a positive constant.]_

Here we adopt the definition of _B_ in AdaQuantFL, but modified it as follows: **Definition 1** (Number of Bits sent from client _m_ to the server, _Bm_ ). _The total number of bits that has been sent from client m to the central server until a given time instant is denoted by Bm._ With the definition of _Bm_ , the upper bound of the loss induced by gradient skipping is indicated by the following theorem:
**Theorem 1.** _Under Assumption 1 and 2, the expected gradient loss caused by lazy aggregation has the following upper bound:_
_where σ_[2] _denotes the maximum ∥w∥_[2] 2 _[,][and][d][m][is][a][positive][constant][determined][by][L][m][.][See][the] Appendix for proof details._
For each client _m_ , the optimal _b[k] m_[is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to] zero:

Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as _σ_ and _dm_ can be achieved through dividing _b[k] m_[by] _[ b]_[1] _m_[, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in] (7) if _b_[1] _m_[for each client] _[ m]_[ is chosen as the initial quantization level] _[ b]_[0][.]

**Comparison with AdaQuantFL.** In AdaQuantFL, the optimal quantization level is selected by minimizing the upper bound of the expected squared norm of the gradient _∇f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) of a non-convex objective function, and thus the convergence of AdaQuantFL is guaranteed. However, the proposed AQUILA is proven to be more efficient with sufficiently small learning rates as shown in Theorem 2. Besides, it is theoretically guaranteed that the squared norm of the global gradient _∇f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) is also able to converge under AQUILA’ s adaptive quantization criterion developed for lazy aggregation.
**Theorem 2.** _Under Assumption 1 and 2, if the learning rate α satisfies α ≤_ (1 _/ML_ ) _·_ �2 _Pξ/L, the following inequality holds:_

_where ξ and P are both positive constants. See the Appendix for proof details._

Theorem 2 indicates that with appropriate learning rate, the proposed AQUILA uses less number of bits per communication compared to AdaQuantFL.

**Convergence analysis.** The Lyapunov function of AQUILA is defined in the same way as LAQ and AQG (Mao et al., 2021):

where _**θ**[∗]_ is the optimal solution of min _**θ** f_ ( _**θ**_ ).

Convergence guarantee of federated learning with lazy aggregation has been well discussed in Sun et al. (2020). More specifically, both the objective residual _f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) _− f_ ( _**θ**[∗]_ ) and the parameter differences term in Lyapunov function are guaranteed to descend along with the training process. Therefore, the squared norm of the gradient _∇f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) is also guaranteed to converge based on the _L_ -smoothness assumption which results in: _∥∇f_ ( _**θ**[k]_ ) _∥_ 2[2] _[≤]_[2] _[L]_[[] _[f]_[(] _**[θ]**[k]_[)] _[ −][f]_[(] _**[θ]**[∗]_[)]][.]

## 4 EXPERIMENTS AND DISCUSSION

## 4.1 EXPERIMENT SETUP

**Dataset.** In this paper, we evaluate our method with MNIST and CIFAR10 dataset, considering both IID and Non-IID data distribution. To simulate Non-IID situation, each client is assigned with two classes of data at most and the amount of data for each class is balanced.

**Parameters.** We set total client number _M_ = 10, and follow the settings in LAQ, where _D_ = 10 and _ξ_ 1 = _ξ_ 2 = _· · ·_ = _ξD_ = 0 _._ 8 _/D_ . In terms of initial quantization level, a low level _b_ 0 = 2 is chosen for simpler tasks with MNIST, and a larger level _b_ 0 = 6 is selected for more complex tasks with CIFAR10. For both AdaQuantFL and AQUILA, we set an upperbound for quantization level as _bmax_ = 16 in case that the level grows too high.
**Training.** We train a CNN with MNIST and a Resnet18 network with CIFAR10. The hyperparameters of our experiments are shown in Table 1 in Appendix.

We first evaluate our proposed AQUILA with homogeneous settings where all the local models share the same architecture as the global model. The performance of AQUILA is compared with several state-of-the-art methods including FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017), QSGD (Alistarh et al., 2017), AdaQuantFL, fixed-bit LAQ and the naive combination of AdaQuantFL with LAQ. For the choice of quantization level for fixed-bit LAQ, since AQUILA’s initial level _b_ 0 is 2 for MNIST and 6 for CIFAR10, we compare AQUILA with LAQ-2 and LAQ-6 for MNIST and CIFAR 10 respectively. Besides, the performance of LAQ with the upperbound _bmax_ = 16 is also evaluated.

We also evaluate our proposed AQUILA with HeteroFL (Diao et al., 2020), where the local models trained at clients’ side are heterogeneous. Assume the global model at iteration _k_ is _θg[k]_[and its size] is _dg · hg_ , then the local model of each client _m_ can be selected by _θm[k]_[=] _[θ] g[k]_[[:] _[ d][m][,]_[:] _[ h][m]_[]][,][where] _dm_ = _rmdg_ and _hm_ = _rmhg_ respectively. In this paper, we choose three various model complexity levels _r_ = _{a, b, c}_ = _{_ 1 _,_ 0 _._ 5 _,_ 0 _._ 25 _}_ .

Fig. 3 shows the _training loss vs total transmitted bits_ curve of experiments with IID MNIST / CIFAR10 for homogeneous model architecture, IID MNIST / CIFAR10 for 100%-50% heterogeneous model architecture, and Non-IID MNIST / CIFAR10 for 100%-25% heterogeneous model architecture. The corresponding _transmitted bits vs steps_ curves of the above experiment settings are shown in Fig. 4, which represents how many bits are transmitted in each step. Methods without adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation like FedAvg and QSGD are not included in Fig. 4 for simplicity. All the other experiment results are provided in Appendix, with Fig. 5 to Fig. 9 for homogeneous models, and Fig. 10 to Fig. 19 for heterogeneous models.

## 4.2 PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS

In this part, we analyze the performance of AQUILA with various experiment settings including Non-IID data distribution and heterogeneous model architecture. From figures in this paper, we can observe that:

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

- AQUILA achieves a significant transmission reduction on both MNIST and CIFAR10 as compared to AdaQuantFL and the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL. For instance, AQUILA saves 65.98% of transmitted bits compared with AdaQuantFL and 62.05% compared with the naive combination of LAQ and AdaQuantFL in the IID scenario with CIFAR10 dataset as shown in Fig. 3d, and other figures all show an obvious reduction in terms of the total transmitted bits required for convergence. Fig. 4 verifies that the quantization level selected by AQUILA will not continuously increase during training like AdaQuantFL and LAQ with AdaQuantFL.

- Similarly, AQUILA outperforms all fixed-level LAQ in terms of overall transmitted bits for both MNIST and CIFAR10, as shown in Fig. 3. For example, Fig. 3a indicates that AQUILA reduces 42.6% of total transmitted bits compared with LAQ-2 and 92.32% compared with LAQ-16 in MNIST. The transmission reduction is 38.76% for LAQ-6 and 78.09% for LAQ-16 in CIFAR10 as shown in Fig. 3e. Besides, Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 indicate that although LAQ with fixed but low quantization level like LAQ-2 and LAQ-4 sometimes transmit smaller amount of bits per step compared with AQUILA, they suffer from lower accuracy and slower convergence. It further verifies the necessity and effectiveness of our well-designed adaptive quantization criterion which achieves fast convergence with similar low-bit transmission but without degradation of the model performance.

- AQUILA also works well with heterogeneous local models. With the two various ways of distributing the heterogeneous local models in this paper, our proposed AQUILA still outperforms other methods by significantly reducing overall transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model accuracy. Please refer to Fig. 10 to Fig. 19 in the Appendix for more detailed information. These experimental results in nonhomogeneous FL settings prove that our proposed AQUILA can be used in a more general and complicated federated learning scenarios.

AdaQuantFL+LAQ represents the naive combination of AdaQuantFL and LAQ. For heterogeneous model architecture, 100%-50% implies that half of clients have 100% of global model, whereas the other half of clients just share 50% _∗_ 50% of the global model. Similarly, 100%-25% means the other half of clients just share 25% _∗_ 25% of the global model. Particularly, we zoom in the end of the curves to better compare AQUILA with other methods.

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## 5 CONCLUSIONS AND FUTURE WORK

This paper proposes a communication efficient FL framework to simultaneously adjust two mutually-dependent degrees of freedom: communication frequency and quantization level. With the close cooperation of the novel adaptive quantization and adjusted lazy aggregation strategy developed in this paper, the proposed AQUILA has been proven to be capable of reducing the transmitted bits while maintaining the same convergence property and model performance compared against existing methods both theoretically and experimentally. The evaluation with Non-IID data distribution and various heterogeneous model architectures demonstrates that AQUILA is compatible to existing FL settings. Future works include further improvements and theoretical guarantee for FL systems with heterogeneity.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents AQUILA, a framework combining lazy aggregation with adaptive quantization to reduce communication overhead in Federated Learning. The core idea—jointly optimizing communication frequency and bit-depth—is well-motivated and addresses a known limitation where increasing quantization precision in existing methods (like AdaQuantFL) can inadvertently trigger more frequent transmissions in lazily-aggregated schemes. However, there are significant technical concerns regarding the derivation of the main theorem (Theorem 1) and its resulting bit-selection criterion, as well as an unclear definition of 'gradient loss' which is central to the optimization objective.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper presents a well-motivated and technically sound framework, AQUILA, that addresses an important gap in communication-efficient federated learning by jointly optimizing quantization levels and communication frequency. The theoretical derivation is careful, and the experimental evaluation is comprehensive across multiple non-IID and heterogeneous settings. The core ideas are clearly presented, and the synergy between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation is convincingly demonstrated. However, there are a few moderate technical issues in the theoretical justification and inconsistency in bit calculation that need clarification. These do not invalidate the core contributions but affect the precision of the theoretical claims and reproducibility.

comments.title: Incorrect bit count formula for quantized gradient transmission
comments.quote: the number of bits for transmitting a quantized gradient from a client to the central server is Cb = d ⌈ log2( b + 1) ⌉ + d + 32, with 32 bits for ∥ g ∥ 2, 1 bit for each sign ( gi ), and log2( b + 1) bits for each ξi ( g , b ).
comments.explanation: The bit count formula states that each quantization level uses log2(b + 1) bits per coordinate, but then uses the ceiling function ⌈log2(b + 1)⌉ in the total. This is internally inconsistent: the text says 'log2(b + 1) bits for each ξi' but the formula uses the ceiling. More critically, ξi(g,b) is defined as taking values in {0,1,...,b−1}, which requires ⌈log2(b)⌉ bits, not ⌈log2(b+1)⌉. For example, if b=2, ξi ∈ {0,1}, requiring 1 bit, and ⌈log2(2)⌉ = 1, but ⌈log2(3)⌉ = 2 would overcount. The correct number of bits to represent ξi is ⌈log2(b)⌉, not ⌈log2(b+1)⌉. The addition of d bits for signs and 32 bits for norm magnitude is standard and correct. The formula should be Cb = d⌈log2(b)⌉ + d + 32. This error persists in the discussion and could mislead readers about the actual bit cost, especially for small b.

comments.title: Unjustified derivation of optimal quantization level from upper bound
comments.quote: For each client m , the optimal b[k] m is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero: ... Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as σ and dm can be achieved through dividing b[k] m by b[1] m, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7) if b[1] m for each client m is chosen as the initial quantization level b[0].
comments.explanation: The paper derives the optimal b by differentiating the upper bound in (9) with respect to b, but (9) contains b in both the numerator and denominator through q_b and the bit count B_m. However, q_b—the quantization variance factor—is itself a function of b, and this dependence is not accounted for in the derivative. Standard stochastic quantization analysis (e.g., in QSGD) shows that q_b ≈ d / b or similar inverse dependence. Ignoring this functional dependence when taking ∂/∂b leads to an incorrect optimization condition. The justification for dropping unknowns σ and d_m by normalization against b[1]_m is also insufficient: while this removes constants, it assumes that the ratio b[k]_m / b[1]_m captures the optimal scaling, which requires additional argument about the evolution of gradient norms or training dynamics. This step skips a non-trivial justification, especially since (7) is central to the method.

comments.title: Inconsistency in parameter specification between text and context
comments.quote: We set total client number M = 10, and follow the settings in LAQ, where D = 10 and ξ1 = ξ2 = · · · = ξD = 0.8/D .
comments.explanation: The paper states that ξd are constant weights used in the LAQ criterion (4), and sets D=10, implying there are 10 such weights. However, M=10 clients are used, and the weights ξd are indexed by d=1 to D, not by client m. The criterion (4) uses sum over d of ξd ||θ[k] − θ[k−d]||^2, so D refers to the number of past iterations considered, not the number of clients. The setting ξd = 0.8/D for d=1 to D is reasonable, but the description 'follow the settings in LAQ' is ambiguous because the original LAQ paper may define D differently. More importantly, D=10 means memory of past 10 global models, which is plausible, but the text does not clarify D is a temporal window, not client-related. This could confuse a reader into thinking ξd are client-specific. However, from the context of (4), it’s clear D is a time horizon, so the issue is more about clarity than mathematical correctness.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition and use of B_m
comments.quote: Definition 1 (Number of Bits sent from client m to the server, Bm ). The total number of bits that has been sent from client m to the central server until a given time instant is denoted by Bm.
comments.explanation: Definition 1 defines B_m as cumulative bits over time, but then in Theorem 1, B_m appears in the denominator of a per-round convergence bound. This suggests B_m is being used as a per-communication bit cost, not cumulative. There is a mismatch between the definition and usage. The upper bound (9) contains B_m in the denominator within a term that appears to represent per-step gradient error, which only makes sense if B_m is bits per message, not total lifetime bits. The authors likely mean B_m as the bits transmitted in that step, consistent with C_b earlier, but they redefine it as cumulative here, creating confusion. Since the optimization in (10) treats B_m as a variable to optimize (via b), it must be per-communication. The reuse of B_m for a different meaning violates standard notation practice and risks serious misunderstanding.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents AQUILA, a novel federated learning framework that jointly optimizes quantization levels and communication frequency. The core idea of minimizing gradient loss to select quantization bits is sound and well-motivated. Theoretical analysis provides convergence guarantees, and experiments demonstrate significant communication reductions (up to 50%) while maintaining accuracy. However, several technical issues require clarification: notation inconsistencies in Algorithm 1, incomplete theoretical derivations in Theorem 1, underspecified assumptions, and vague experimental details for heterogeneous models. The convergence analysis could be strengthened with more details on how adaptive quantization affects LAQ's guarantees.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in quantization operation
comments.quote: Server updates \boldsymbol{\theta}[k+1] by \boldsymbol{\theta}[k] - \alpha \sum_{m=1}^{M} Q_{\hat{b}_{km}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_m[k])
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1 line 22, the quantization operation uses Q_{\hat{b}_{km}}(\hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_m[k]), but earlier in Section 2, quantization was defined as Q_b(\boldsymbol{g}) for a gradient \boldsymbol{g}. This creates confusion about whether we're quantizing the current gradient or the stored gradient from the last transmission. The notation \hat{\boldsymbol{g}}_m[k] typically represents the gradient actually used for aggregation (either new or old), but quantizing it with the current bit-level \hat{b}_{km} when it might be an old gradient quantized with a different level needs clarification.

comments.title: Underspecified quantization variance in Assumption 2
comments.quote: E[\|Q_b(w) - w\|_2^2 | w] \leq q_b \|w\|_2^2, where q_b is a positive constant.
comments.explanation: Assumption 2 states the quantization error bound but doesn't specify how q_b relates to the quantization level b. For the theoretical analysis to be complete and reproducible, the relationship between q_b and b should be explicitly given (e.g., q_b = O(1/b) or similar). Without this, readers cannot verify the theoretical claims or understand how quantization error scales with bit allocation.

comments.title: Undefined parameters in Theorem 2
comments.quote: if the learning rate \alpha satisfies \alpha \leq (1/ML) \cdot \sqrt{2P\xi/L}, the following inequality holds:
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 introduces parameters P and \xi in the learning rate condition without defining them. While these may be defined in the appendix, the theorem statement should be self-contained. This makes it difficult for readers to understand the constraints on \alpha and assess the practicality of this condition.

comments.title: Vague heterogeneous model definition
comments.quote: the local model of each client m can be selected by \boldsymbol{\theta}_m[k] = \boldsymbol{\theta}_g[k][:d_m, :h_m], where d_m = r_m d_g and h_m = r_m h_g respectively.
comments.explanation: The heterogeneous model definition is incomplete. It specifies how to select a subset of parameters from the global model but doesn't explain: (1) How the global model aggregates updates from heterogeneous local models of different sizes, (2) What happens to parameters not included in a client's local model, and (3) How gradients are computed for the missing parameters. These details are crucial for understanding the federated learning process with heterogeneous architectures.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for convergence claim
comments.quote: the squared norm of the global gradient \nabla f(\boldsymbol{\theta}[k]) is also guaranteed to converge based on the L-smoothness assumption which results in: \|\nabla f(\boldsymbol{\theta}[k])\|_2^2 \leq 2L[f(\boldsymbol{\theta}[k]) - f(\boldsymbol{\theta}[\ast])].
comments.explanation: The convergence analysis claims that under AQUILA, the gradient norm converges based solely on LAQ's analysis and L-smoothness. However, AQUILA modifies both the quantization criterion and the communication threshold. The paper doesn't show how these modifications affect the convergence proof. More justification is needed to demonstrate that the adaptive quantization doesn't break LAQ's convergence guarantees.

comments.title: Unclear percentage notation in Figure 3 caption
comments.quote: 100%-50% implies that half of clients have 100% of global model, whereas the other half of clients just share 50% * 50% of the global model.
comments.explanation: The caption uses unclear notation '50% * 50%' to describe model size. Based on the heterogeneous model definition using r_m = {1, 0.5, 0.25}, this likely means 50% width and 50% height (so 25% total parameters), but the notation is ambiguous. Should be clarified as '50% width and 50% height' or '25% of total parameters'.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a coherent framework (AQUILA) for addressing communication efficiency in Federated Learning by jointly optimizing quantization levels and lazy aggregation frequency. The authors identify a genuine conflict between adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation objectives, and the proposed heuristic logic is sound. However, the paper suffers from underspecified mathematical notation and incomplete definitions in the technical section, specifically regarding the derivation of the adaptive quantization criterion. While the experimental results are promising, the theoretical section is difficult to verify because critical variables like 'd_m' are introduced without explicit definitions or bounds.

comments.title: Underspecified notation in Theorem 1
comments.quote: where $\sigma^2$ denotes the maximum $\|w\|_2^2$, and $d_m$ is a positive constant determined by $L_m$.
comments.explanation: The theorem introduces $d_m$ as a 'positive constant determined by $L_m$', but this is insufficient for a formal derivation. In the context of convergence analysis for non-convex Federated Learning, the relationship between $d_m$ and the smoothness constant $L_m$ (or other local parameters) is essential to understand the contribution to the gradient loss bound. Without an explicit definition (e.g., whether it relates to the variance of the local gradients or the heterogeneity across clients), the result is difficult to parse.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of the aggregated gradient
comments.quote: where $M_k^0$ denotes the subset of clients that skip the new gradient update and reuse the old quantized gradient at iteration $k$.
comments.explanation: Equation (5) and the accompanying text use $M_k^0$ to describe the subset of clients whose gradients are skipped. However, in section 3.1 and the algorithm, the criteria for skipping (8) is client-specific. It is ambiguous whether $M_k^0$ is a set defined by the server based on received signals or a set defined by the clients themselves. This ambiguity makes it unclear how the global parameter $\theta$ is updated when the server does not 'know' which clients skipped their update until the communication phase is complete.

comments.title: Inconsistency between equation (7) and preceding derived bounds
comments.quote: computes the optimal local quantization level $b_m^{(k)}$ by (7).
comments.explanation: The paper claims criterion (7) is derived from setting the first derivative of an upper bound (9) to zero. However, looking at the transition from Theorem 1 (where the loss depends on $b_m^{(k)}$ and internal variables like $\sigma$ and $d_m$) to (7), the paper asserts that one can simplify the expression by choosing $b_{m}^{(1)}$ as the initial level. This step is a significant leap that lacks a clear justification or derivation showing why the dependence on the Lipschitz-related constants $d_m$ effectively cancels out or becomes negligible for the adaptive selection.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes AQUILA, a federated learning framework that jointly optimizes communication frequency (via lazy aggregation) and quantization levels. The core intuition—balancing bit allocation against gradient skipping loss—is well-motivated, and the experimental results demonstrate clear communication savings across heterogeneous and Non-IID settings. However, the manuscript suffers from significant presentation and theoretical gaps: several key equations (parameter updates, thresholds, upper bounds) are missing due to OCR/extraction issues, the derivation of the adaptive quantization criterion skips critical steps regarding continuous relaxation and discrete rounding, and notation is overloaded in the lazy aggregation threshold. Additionally, the server update rule and learning rate bounds lack standard clarifications. With corrected derivations, explicit averaging conventions, and cleaned notation, the theoretical claims and reproducibility would be substantially strengthened.

comments.title: Notation overloading: dimension vs. summation index in LAQ threshold
comments.quote: where _Q_b_ (ˆ _**g**[k] m[−]_[1] ) is the last quantized upload from client _m_ , _ε_b_ (ˆ _**g**[k] m[−]_[1] ) and _ε_b_ ( _**g**[k] m_[)][ denote quantiza-] tion errors, and _{ξ_d}[D] d_ =1[are some predetermined constant weights.]
comments.explanation: In the description of the lazy aggregation criterion, the symbol _d_ is used simultaneously to represent the model dimension (implicitly in the norm terms and bit count formula _C_b = d⌈log2(b+1)⌉ + d + 32_) and as the index of summation for the predetermined weights _{ξ_d}_{d=1}^D_. This overloading is confusing and mathematically ambiguous: it is unclear whether the threshold scales by the dimension _d_ times the sum of weights, or if the weights are indexed per dimension. Standard LAQ literature (e.g., Sun et al., 2020) typically uses a different index for the sum (e.g., _l_ or _j_) to distinguish it from the gradient dimension. I recommend changing the summation index to avoid conflating two distinct quantities.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for derivation of adaptive quantization criterion (Eq. 7)
comments.quote:
For each client _m_ , the optimal _b[k] m_[is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero: 

 Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as _σ_ and _dm_ can be achieved through dividing _b[k] m_[by] _[ b]_[1] _m_[, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in] (7) if _b_[1] _m_[for each client] _[ m]_[ is chosen as the initial quantization level] _[ b]_[0][.]
comments.explanation: The text claims that Eq. (7) is obtained by taking the first derivative of the upper bound (9), setting it to zero, and then dividing by _b_1^m. This derivation skips several essential steps that prevent verification: (1) the actual form of bound (9) and its derivative are omitted; (2) quantization levels are inherently discrete (integer bits), yet the optimization relies on continuous calculus without explaining the relaxation or justification for the floor/log operations in Eq. (7); (3) the normalization step ('dividing _b_m^k_ by _b_1^m_') is mathematically opaque—dividing a continuous bound by the initial level does not obviously yield the logarithmic gradient-ratio form seen in Eq. (7); and (4) how the final discrete criterion is stabilized or clipped is not shown. Without these intermediate steps, the claim that Eq. (7) minimizes the expected gradient loss is unsubstantiated. Re-deriving or explicitly stating the Lagrangian/continuous relaxation steps would significantly improve theoretical rigor.

comments.title: Underspecified server aggregation rule in Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: The server update step sums the quantized gradients over all _M_ clients, including those who skipped transmission (for whom _ĝ_m^k = ĝ_m^{k-1}_ is reused). However, standard federated averaging and LAQ conventions include a _1/M_ scaling factor: _θ^{k+1} = θ^k - (α/M) ∑_{m=1}^M Q_{b̂_m^k}(ĝ_m^k)_. While it is possible that the learning rate _α_ is pre-scaled to absorb the client count, the algorithm does not specify this convention. Omitting the explicit averaging term or clarifying that _α_ already incorporates _1/M_ may mislead reproducibility efforts or cause hyperparameter mismatch when comparing with baselines like FedAvg or LAQ. I recommend explicitly writing the aggregation rule and noting how _α_ is scaled.

comments.title: Ambiguous learning rate bound in Theorem 2
comments.quote: Theorem 2. Under Assumption 1 and 2, if the learning rate α satisfies α ≤_ (1 _/ML_ ) _·_ 2 _Pξ/L,_ the following inequality holds:_
comments.explanation: The condition on the learning rate appears to contain a typesetting or OCR artifact in the radical: _√2 Pξ/L_. It is unclear whether the square root covers only _2_, _2Pξ_, or the entire fraction _2Pξ/L_. Standard FL convergence theorems typically bound _α_ by terms like _O(1/√(KL))_ or _O(1/(L√K))_. The lack of parentheses or clear scope for the radical makes it impossible to verify if the condition is correctly stated or aligns with standard non-convex convergence guarantees. Clarifying the exact mathematical expression and its derivation scope is necessary for theoretical verification.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents a compelling unification of adaptive quantization and lazy aggregation for Federated Learning, offering a strong experimental and preliminary theoretical contribution. The proposed framework, AQUILA, shows significant promise in reducing communication overheads while maintaining accuracy. However, the review identified a critical logical inconsistency regarding the definition of the AdaQuantFL baseline in the background section versus the theoretical justification in the method section, which undermines the validity of the theoretical comparison. Additionally, there is an ambiguity in the definition of the optimization criterion that could confuse readers, and the abstract understates the experimental performance claims made in the paper.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper tackles an important problem in federated learning—jointly optimizing communication frequency and quantization level—and proposes a framework called AQUILA. However, the main text suffers from several significant gaps that undermine reproducibility and the clarity of the claimed contributions. The modified lazy aggregation criterion (8) is not specified in the main text, leaving the core algorithm incomplete. The derivation of the adaptive quantization rule (7) from the theoretical upper bound is presented only at a high level, with crucial steps omitted, making it impossible to verify. Additionally, Theorem 2 is framed as a bit-efficiency claim but states a convergence bound, and the logical connection is not explained. These issues, while potentially resolved in the appendix, are serious for a self-contained paper.

comments.title: Missing communication criterion (8) in main text
comments.quote:
we modify the communication criterion as follows to avoid the potential expansion of clients group to be skipped:
(8)
ˆ b where [k] m[−] [1] for each client.all the notationsFor detailed development of (8), please refer to the Appendix.are the same as in (4) except the heterogeneous quantization level b[k] m[and]
comments.explanation: The communication criterion (8) is a central component of the AQUILA algorithm—it determines whether a client skips an update. However, the equation is never written out in the main text; only a placeholder appears. The text refers to the appendix for details, but the paper as presented to reviewers should at least state the criterion explicitly. Without it, a reader cannot understand how the lazy aggregation strategy is modified, and the algorithm in the main text is incomplete. This is a major reproducibility concern.

comments.title: Insufficient derivation of adaptive quantization criterion (7)
comments.quote:
For each client m, the optimal b[k] m[is selected by setting the first derivative of the upper bound (9) to zero:
zero:
Therefore, an adaptive quantization criterion which does not rely on unknown parameters such as σ and dm can be achieved through dividing b[k] m[by] b[1] m[, which gives the adaptive selection criterion in (7) if b[1] m[for each client] m[ is chosen as the initial quantization level] b[0][.]
comments.explanation: The derivation skips from 'set first derivative to zero' to the final criterion (7) without showing the intermediate expression, the form of q_b, or how division removes the unknowns. The upper bound (9) is stated only vaguely, and the differentiation is not performed. This is a non-trivial step that requires justification; the reader cannot verify that (7) indeed follows from the optimization. This gap weakens the theoretical foundation of the method.

comments.title: Theorem 2's claim about bit efficiency is unsupported
comments.quote:
Theorem 2. Under Assumption 1 and 2, if the learning rate α satisfies α ≤ (1/ML) ·  2 Pξ/L, the following inequality holds:

where ξ and P are both positive constants. See the Appendix for proof details.

Theorem 2 indicates that with appropriate learning rate, the proposed AQUILA uses less number of bits per communication compared to AdaQuantFL.
comments.explanation: The inequality in Theorem 2 is a convergence bound on the expected squared norm of the gradient; it does not directly compare the number of bits transmitted. The claim that this implies 'less number of bits per communication' is an overstatement without additional reasoning linking the bound to bit count. No derivation or argument is provided in the main text to support this interpretation. The reader is left to guess how convergence properties translate to bit savings, which is a serious logical gap.

comments.title: Ambiguity in notation for quantization errors and upper bound (9)
comments.quote: εb (ˆ g [k] m[−] [1] ) and εb (g [k] m[)] [denote quantiza-] tion errors
comments.explanation: It is unclear what ε_b(·) represents exactly. Is it the expected squared quantization error, the variance, or the actual realized error? The definition is missing, and this ambiguity propagates into the LAQ criterion (4) and the modified criterion (8). The upper bound (9) also uses σ^2 and d_m without clear definitions (e.g., 'σ^2 denotes the maximum ∥w∥_2^2' is vague—maximum over what set?). These imprecisions make it difficult to follow the theoretical development.

comments.title: Underspecified parameter D and constants ξ_d in LAQ criterion
comments.explanation: The parameter D and the weights ξ_d appear in the LAQ communication criterion (4), but their meaning is not explained in the main text. D likely represents a window size or number of past steps, but the paper does not specify how it is chosen or what role it plays. While the appendix may clarify, the main text should provide a brief justification or reference to the original LAQ paper to aid understanding.
